prompt:
The music-library prototype has been living as a pile of dicts and it's time to put it on a real ORM. We've standardized on SQLAlchemy 2.x — and I mean actual 2.0 style: DeclarativeBase, Mapped[...] / mapped_column, select() statements. If I see a legacy Query anywhere I'll cry. Everything goes in one module, music.py; test_music.py is the acceptance contract and bash run_verify.sh runs it out of the project venv. Nothing is installed on this machine yet, so first set up our usual local .venv from the pinned requirements.txt.

The shape of it:
- Models Artist, Album, Track, Playlist, PlaylistEntry on tables artists/albums/tracks/playlists/playlist_entries. An artist has many albums (artist names unique), an album has many tracks (each track has title, seconds, and a 1-based position within the album), and a playlist orders tracks through PlaylistEntry rows that carry a position — a Playlist's .entries come back sorted by position no matter what order they were attached in.
- Deleting an album must take its tracks with it; nothing may be left dangling.
- make_engine() returns an Engine on in-memory SQLite; init_db(engine) creates the schema.
- add_album(session, artist_name, title, year, tracks) with tracks as (title, seconds) pairs: reuses the artist row if the name exists, creates it otherwise, numbers positions from 1 in the given order, returns the Album. Session discipline matters: helpers NEVER commit — the caller owns the transaction (flush is fine).
- Three query functions, each returning plain lists of tuples: album_runtimes(session, artist_name) -> [(album_title, total_seconds)] ordered by album year; tracks_longer_than(session, min_seconds) -> [(artist_name, album_title, track_title)] for tracks STRICTLY longer, ordered by seconds descending then title ascending; playlist_titles(session, playlist_name) -> track titles in playlist order (unknown names give []).

Done when bash run_verify.sh passes.
